_doc: Golden set for the resolver agent. Each case has an optional `mode` field: 'deterministic' (run only with --llm none), 'llm' (run only with --llm gemini), or 'both' / absent (run in both modes). The runner picks which set to load via the --llm flag — see tests/eval/resolver/run_eval.py. Catalog fixture: tests/eval/resolver/fixtures/catalog.sql layered on top of init_complete_database.sql. Assertion shapes: resolved_sku (exact), resolved_sku_in (any-of, for cases where multiple outcomes are acceptable), has_resolution_error (true).

cases._note: Direct SKU lookup, no fuzzy logic involved. Runs in both modes — exact SKU should never trigger LLM.
User says 'pulsera negra' — must map to BC-BRACELET-BLACK. In deterministic mode the negra→black translation table handles it; in LLM mode the disambiguator picks BLACK from the multi-candidate set.
user_input contains the variant 'dorada' → detect_variant_hints triggers → must resolve to GOLD bracelet.
ADD_STOCK with two items; both must resolve, in the order the router emitted them.

Bare 'pulsera' yields zero candidates from fuzzy_match_with_scores (the word is in the skip list of meaningful_words), so resolve_product_reference fires the standard error. Holds in both modes — branch A0 (no candidates) bypasses the LLM disambiguator entirely.
User says 'arcoiris' — must match BC-BRACELET-RAINBOW via specific descriptor.

User says 'pulsera celeste' — no celeste product. The deterministic path emits resolution_error. Excluded from --llm gemini because Gemini equates celeste≈turquesa and resolves to TURQUOISE (real prod behavior, not a bug — but the case asserts the deterministic behavior, so we run it deterministic-only). LLM-mode rejection pattern is covered by llm_disambig_pulsera_xs_rejects_underspec and the two collar_*_rejects_only_match cases.

Branch B (single low-confidence). 'collar artesanal clasico' yields one candidate (BC-NECKLACE-CLASSIC, score 0.67). With the prompt allowing null, the LLM rejects because 'artesanal' is not present in the candidate's name. Catalog-level fact ('todo el catálogo es artesanal') is not visible to the LLM, so it treats 'artesanal' as a discriminating attribute and surfaces resolution_error rather than guess. Asking back is the safer prod behavior.
Branch B (single low-confidence). 'aros silver' yields one candidate (BC-EARRINGS-SILVER, score 0.50) — silver is not in the translation table but the LLM should map it to plateados.

Branch B (single low-confidence). 'collar de plata' yields BC-NECKLACE-CLASSIC (0.50) only because 'collar' overlaps with the necklace's name — but 'plata' (silver) is not an attribute of the Classic necklace. With the prompt allowing null, the LLM should reject the only candidate and surface a resolution_error rather than guess.

Branch B (single low-confidence). 'collar largo' yields BC-NECKLACE-CLASSIC (0.50) only because 'collar' overlaps — but 'largo' (long) is not an attribute of the Classic necklace. Same rejection pattern as collar_de_plata.
Branch C (multi-candidate). 10 candidates with overlap on 'pulsera'; turquesa is the discriminator. LLM must pick TURQUOISE.
Branch C (multi-candidate). LLM must pick PURPLE.
Branch C (multi-candidate). LLM must pick WHITE.

Branch C (multi-candidate). 'pulsera xs' yields all 10 bracelets on 'pulsera' overlap — XS is not a real size variant of any of them. With the prompt allowing null, the LLM should reject rather than guess a color when the user specified a size that doesn't exist in the catalog.

Branch C (multi-candidate). 'pulsera de color' is a generic qualifier — every bracelet variant is 'colored'. With the prompt allowing null, the LLM should reject and ask for the specific color rather than invent a creative interpretation like equating 'colored' with 'rainbow'. The prompt explicitly flags 'generic qualifier' as a reject reason.

Branch C (multi-candidate). 'pulsera dorada arcoiris' contains two real but mutually exclusive attributes (GOLD and RAINBOW are separate SKUs). With the prompt allowing null, the LLM rejects because no single candidate has both attributes. This is the correct outcome — surfacing resolution_error triggers a clarification flow upstream rather than silently picking one of two equally likely interpretations.
